steps.title: Publication Identity
steps.description: Let's start with the basics — what is your publication called and where will it live?

fields.label: Publication Name
fields.help: What's your publication called? This appears in the header, browser tab, and social cards. Examples: "CLIMATE DOSSIER", "AI WHISTLEBLOWER", "CYBER PULSE"

fields.label: Full Title
fields.help: Full title with tagline. Example: "CLIMATE DOSSIER — Daily Climate Policy Briefing"

fields.label: Tagline
fields.help: One-line description for search results and social shares. Example: "Daily intelligence on corporate AI accountability — whistleblower cases, transparency, and oversight."

fields.label: Site URL
fields.help: Where will the site live? Example: "https://climatedossier.org"

fields.label: Publisher Name
fields.help: Your organization name. Example: "Climate Action Network"

fields.label: Publisher URL
fields.help: Your organization's website. Example: "https://climatenetwork.org"

steps.title: Your Audience
steps.description: This is the most important section. It shapes how every story is evaluated and written. Think about who you want reading this every morning.
steps.hint: We initially described our audience too technically and the pipeline produced jargon-heavy copy. Describing the reader as "a curious professional reading over morning coffee" dramatically improved readability. Write for who you want to reach, not who you already have.

fields.label: Who Reads This?
fields.help: List 2-4 reader types, comma-separated. Example: "climate policy advisors, environmental journalists, ESG analysts, sustainability professionals"

fields.label: Publication Type
fields.help: How would you describe this publication in a sentence fragment? Example: "climate policy intelligence briefing", "corporate accountability watchdog"

fields.label: Topic Label
fields.help: Your topic in 2-4 words. Used throughout the pipeline. Example: "climate policy", "AI accountability", "cybersecurity"

fields.label: Key Question
fields.help: The single analytical question every story is evaluated against. This shapes how importance is scored. Example: "How does this affect the trajectory of global decarbonization? Think through policy incentives, corporate behavior, and public pressure dynamics."

fields.label: Community Label
fields.help: What do you call your community? Example: "the climate policy community", "the cybersecurity community"

fields.label: Reader Profile
fields.help: Describe your ideal reader in 3-5 sentences. Who are they? What's their expertise level? What should they take away? Example: "You are writing for smart professionals who follow climate policy but are not scientists. Think: a policy advisor at a national environment ministry, a reporter covering COP negotiations, or an ESG analyst screening investments. They should understand WHY a development matters without needing atmospheric chemistry."

steps.title: Categories
steps.description: Every story gets classified into one of your categories. These appear as filter buttons on your site and organize the editorial workflow. Pick categories that cover your topic's major dimensions without overlapping.
steps.hint: Each category should have enough daily volume to regularly appear (at least 1-2 stories per day). If two categories always contain the same stories, merge them.

fields.label: Categories
fields.help: Define your categories. Each one becomes a filter button on your site. Example: Short Label "Policy", Full Name "Climate Policy & Governance".

fields.label: Short Label
fields.help: Short label for the filter button. Example: "Policy", "Science", "Finance"

fields.label: Full Name
fields.help: Full category name. Example: "Climate Policy & Governance"

fields.label: Auto-Tags
fields.help: Keywords that help the pipeline sort stories into this category. If a story mentions any of these words, it's a strong signal it belongs here. At least 2 tags, comma-separated, letters and hyphens only. Example: "governance, regulation, legislation, treaty"

steps.title: Editorial Team
steps.description: Your publication has AI editors who each review every story through their domain expertise. Each editor matches one of your categories, plus a Spotlight editor who finds interesting media content like podcasts, videos, and demos. The more specific you describe each editor's expertise, the sharper their reviews will be.
steps.hint: Vague expertise ("you are an expert in policy") produces generic reviews. Listing specific organizations, sub-areas, and judgment criteria produces sharp, domain-aware assessments. The more specific, the better.

fields.label: Editors
fields.help: One editor per category, plus a Spotlight editor. We'll pre-fill these from your categories — just add the expertise descriptions.

fields.label: Editor Name
fields.help: Editor title. Example: "Policy Editor", "Science Editor", "Spotlight Editor"

fields.label: Category
fields.help: Which of your categories does this editor cover? Use the short label (e.g. "Policy", "Science"). For the Spotlight Editor, use "spotlight". Letters, numbers, hyphens, and spaces only.

fields.label: Is Spotlight Editor
fields.help: Check this for the Spotlight editor — the one that finds podcasts, videos, demos, and other interesting non-news content.

fields.label: Expertise
fields.help: Write 1-2 paragraphs: who they are, what sub-areas they cover (as a bullet list), and what they notice that generalists miss. Example: "You are the Policy Editor — an expert in climate governance and international negotiations. You track COP proceedings, UNFCCC updates, EU Green Deal implementation, and US climate policy. You understand whether policy moves are substantive or performative."
fields.placeholder: You are the ... Editor — an expert in...

steps.title: Sentiment Tracker
steps.description: Your publication includes a sentiment tracker that measures whether things in your domain are getting better or worse over time. Every story gets scored as positive, negative, or neutral. Name your tracker and define what those directions mean.

fields.label: Tracker Name
fields.help: Give your tracker a name — it appears in the navigation and on charts. Think of it as a metric that captures the direction of your domain. Example: "Climate Risk Index", "Trust Score", "Momentum Tracker"

fields.label: Tracker Subtitle
fields.help: A longer name used in page titles and metadata. Example: "Climate Policy Sentiment Index", "AI Accountability Trust Score"

fields.label: Positive Signal Label
fields.help: What do you call it when things improve? This label shows up next to positive stories. Example: "progress points", "trust points", "green points"

fields.label: Negative Signal Label
fields.help: What do you call it when things get worse? This label shows up next to negative stories. Example: "risk points", "erosion points", "red points"

fields.label: Tracker Nav Label
fields.help: Short label for the navigation tab — what users click to see the tracker. Example: "Tracker", "Risk Index", "Pulse"

fields.label: What Positive Means
fields.help: In one sentence, what does a positive signal mean in your domain? Example: "Decarbonization is accelerating — policy, finance, or technology moving in the right direction."

fields.label: What Negative Means
fields.help: In one sentence, what does a negative signal mean? Example: "Decarbonization is slowing — rollbacks, delays, or new obstacles."

fields.label: Fallback Sentiment Question
fields.help: When a story's sentiment isn't obvious, the pipeline uses this question as a tiebreaker. Frame it as a yes/no question about direction. Example: "Is the world's trajectory toward net-zero getting better or worse?"
fields.placeholder: Is the situation getting better or worse?

fields.label: Tracker Concept URL
fields.help: Optional link to a page explaining your metric concept (Wikipedia, blog post, etc.).

fields.label: Positive Scoring — What Does It Look Like?
fields.help: Describe what positive signals look like in your domain. Give 2-3 concrete examples of stories your audience would instantly recognize as good news. Example: "New binding emissions targets signed into law. Major fossil fuel company announces accelerated phase-out timeline. Record-breaking renewable energy deployment in a G20 country."

fields.label: Negative Scoring — What Does It Look Like?
fields.help: Describe what negative signals look like. Give 2-3 concrete examples. Example: "Climate legislation repealed or weakened. Major emitter misses its own targets. New fossil fuel infrastructure approved despite net-zero pledges."

fields.label: Neutral Scoring — What Counts as Neutral?
fields.help: Describe what counts as neutral — neither clearly positive nor negative. Example: "Routine data releases without clear trend direction. Personnel changes at agencies without policy implications. Conference announcements or procedural updates."

steps.title: Sentiment Rules
steps.description: For each of your categories, define rules that tell the pipeline when something is positive, negative, or neutral. Focus on the non-obvious cases — the pipeline already gets the obvious ones right. Think: "When X happens in this category, is that good or bad for my domain?"
steps.hint: We discovered a strong negativity bias in early builds. The model treated all serious stories as negative, even defensive improvements. Adding explicit "this is positive because..." rules fixed this. If you don't define what's positive, the pipeline assumes serious = negative.

optional.label: I don't need custom sentiment rules
optional.consequences: Without custom sentiment rules, the pipeline will use general-purpose sentiment analysis. It works well for obvious cases but may misjudge domain-specific signals — for example, treating new regulation as negative when it's actually progress in your field.

fields.label: Sentiment Rules
fields.help: For each category, define rules that tell the pipeline when something is positive, negative, or neutral. Focus on the non-obvious cases — the pipeline already gets the obvious ones right.

steps.title: Social Profiles
steps.description: If your field has active social media discussion, the pipeline can monitor key voices across platforms. This feeds into social signals shown to editors and the daily community pulse.

optional.label: I don't need social monitoring
optional.consequences: Without social profiles, the pipeline won't track community discussion or include social signals in story scoring. You can always add these later.

fields.label: Bluesky Handles
fields.help: List key researchers, journalists, and advocates on Bluesky. Example: "climatescientist.bsky.social"

fields.label: Bluesky Handle
fields.help: Full Bluesky handle. Example: "climatescientist.bsky.social"

fields.label: X.com Handles
fields.help: List key voices on X (formerly Twitter). Example: "@climatejournalist"

fields.label: X Handle
fields.help: X handle with or without @. Example: "@climatejournalist" or "climatejournalist"

fields.label: LinkedIn Profiles
fields.help: LinkedIn profile URLs for key voices in your field.

fields.label: LinkedIn URL
fields.help: Full LinkedIn profile URL. Example: "https://linkedin.com/in/climate-expert"

steps.title: Key Organizations & Triage Topics
steps.description: Two quick lists: the organizations that matter most in your field, and the topic areas your publication should monitor.

optional.label: I'll add these later
optional.consequences: Without key organizations, stories won't get score bonuses for mentioning important players. Without triage topics, the pipeline will use your categories and key question for filtering, which works but is less precise.

fields.label: Key Organizations
fields.help: Stories mentioning these organizations get a score bonus. List 10-15 whose actions are most newsworthy. Don't list too many — if everything gets a bonus, nothing does.

fields.label: Organization Name
fields.help: Full organization name. Example: "United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change", "International Energy Agency"

fields.label: Triage Topics
fields.help: These are the first filter — anything not matching these topics gets discarded before the editors even see it. Be generous — better to flag too many than miss something. Aim for 4-6 topics.

fields.label: Topic
fields.help: A short phrase defining what's relevant. Example: "Climate litigation and legal precedents", "Corporate emissions reporting and greenwashing"

steps.title: Sources
steps.description: List the RSS feeds your publication should monitor daily. Start with 20-30 high-quality sources — you can always add more later.
steps.hint: A good mix: 5-10 primary sources (orgs that make news), 5-10 analysis (newsletters, blogs), 5-10 media (journalists covering your beat), 5-10 wire services. Start with 20-30 high-quality sources. A smaller set of trusted sources produces better output than 100 mediocre feeds full of noise.

fields.label: RSS Sources
fields.help: Add your RSS feed sources. To verify a feed works, try visiting the URL — it should show XML content.

batchInput.jsonExample: {"name": "Carbon Brief", "url": "https://www.carbonbrief.org/feed", "tags": "policy, science", "notes": "UK-based climate science and policy analysis"}

fields.label: Source Name
fields.help: Publication or organization name. Example: "Carbon Brief", "Reuters Climate"

fields.label: Feed URL
fields.help: RSS feed URL. Tip: try adding /feed, /rss, /feed.xml, or /index.xml to the site URL. Example: "https://www.carbonbrief.org/feed"

fields.label: Category Tags
fields.help: Comma-separated tags matching your category labels. Letters, numbers, hyphens, and spaces only. Example: "policy, governance"

fields.label: Notes
fields.help: Any notes about this source — for your reference, not used by the pipeline.

steps.title: About Page & Final Details
steps.description: Almost done! Write a few sentences for your About page and provide any final details.

optional.label: I'll write the about page later
optional.consequences: Without about page content, we'll generate placeholder text based on your other answers. You can always edit this later on your site.

fields.label: What Is This?
fields.help: Describe your publication in 2-3 sentences for a first-time visitor. What will they find here and why should they care?

fields.label: About the Tracker
fields.help: Explain your sentiment tracker — what it measures and why it matters. 2-3 sentences.

fields.label: Built By
fields.help: Describe your organization in 1-2 sentences. Who are you and why did you build this?

fields.label: LLMs Description
fields.help: One-sentence description for AI crawlers (llms.txt). Example: "Daily climate policy intelligence briefing curated from 60+ sources."
